original_abstract:
Since the launch of the 1998 Reformation movement by Indonesian students and civil society, nearly every sector of social and intellectual life has undergone a profound transformation. This impact is particularly visible in religious understanding and practice. Prior to this era, the Indonesian public lived under the constraints of a status quo that enforced a strict uniformity of thought and movement, including within the religious sphere. However, as the Reformation dismantled these rigid structures, the long-standing culture of uniformity dissolved, paving the way for a diverse array of new religious perspectives. 

This shift has brought various modern ideologies—such as Islamic liberalism, fundamentalism, and moderatism—to the forefront of national discourse. In this changing landscape, the study of moderate Islam in Indonesia has gained significant momentum. This research seeks to identify the historical and educational foundations of this moderate tradition, specifically locating its roots within the *Pesantren* (Islamic boarding school) system, which serves as a vital institution for fostering balanced religious development.
humanized_abstract:
For many in Indonesia it will be hard to believe that it has been almost 25 years since a group of student activists together with members of Indonesian civil society launched the 1998 Reformation movement which has changed virtually every level of Indonesian society and life. From politics to social affairs, from culture to education and even religion one can barely find the same way of life as that prior to the fall of Suharto’s autocratic regime in 1998. In fact since then Indonesian society has slowly but steadily developed into being a truly pluralistic and more open society. Religiously speaking too Indonesia has gone through a vast change when compared with the monolithic way of religion prior to the Reformation. During those days in Indonesia people had to tread the same path and perform similar rituals without being allowed to introduce even the minutest variation. Hence in Indonesia there was no real choice as to which creed to believe in and religion was the only formal organisation in the country that did allow some scope for creativity and different views and this was further restricted within the mosques. However since the launching of the Reformation in 1998 people in Indonesia have undergone a dramatic change in the way they understand religion and worship. In the present time in Indonesia there are a myriad of religious views, philosophies and approaches being introduced which is an improvement on the previous rigidity.

The new political reality has made the spread of modern ideologies such as Islamic liberalism, fundamentalism and moderatism inevitable. The concept of moderate Islam in Indonesia is currently being intensively deliberated. It therefore needs to be elaborated in terms of its historical and educational background in order to determine when and where moderate Islam in Indonesia existed and was taught. This study aims to examine the backgrounds of moderate Islam in Indonesia, by looking at the historical and educational backgrounds of Pesantren (Madrasah Al-Sunnah) students as the core of the Islamic educational system in Indonesia that nurtures students with balanced Islamic values.

document.input:
Since the launch of the 1998 Reformation movement by Indonesian students and civil society, nearly every sector of social and intellectual life has undergone a profound transformation. This impact is particularly visible in religious understanding and practice.
